summary:
Aspero is a coastal fishing and ceremonial site located at the mouth of the Supe River in present-day Peru, occupied from approximately 3500 to 2500 BCE. It is one of the earliest and largest known monumental sites in the Americas, featuring at least six large platform mounds built from stone and an ancient technology of knotted fiber net bags (shicras) used to transport and stack fill material during construction. Aspero was a coastal counterpart to the inland [[event:37|Norte Chico]] center of Caral, connected by trade along the Supe River valley.

Excavations at Aspero have uncovered evidence of a society organized around maritime resource exploitation — anchovies, sardines, clams, and other marine species — rather than irrigation agriculture. Elite burials have been found within the platform mounds, including a female burial with elaborate textile and gourd ornaments, suggesting social stratification and possibly female leadership roles. The symbiotic relationship between Aspero's coastal fishing economy and Caral's inland agricultural economy represents an early example of complementary regional specialization supporting the rise of social complexity in the ancient Americas.
